reasoning: The Hittite phrase `nuza eter ekuer` translates to "And they ate and drank." By comparing this to `naإ،ta palhan hإ«mandan ekuer` ("And they drank all the kettles"), we can determine that `ekuer` means "drank". Therefore, `eter` must mean "ate".

The text contains `hatteإ،naz` in the phrase `hatteإ،naz إ،arؤپ kalliإ،ta`, translated as "from the hole". Later, `hatteإ،naإ،` appears in a context translated as "into the hole". This indicates that the root `hatteإ،إ،-` or `hatteإ،n-` means "hole". Thus, `hatteإ،إ،ar` corresponds to "hole".

The word `أ؛it` appears twice in the text. First in `hupaإ،iyaإ،إ،a أ؛it`, which translates to "And Hupasiyas came", and second in `imaإ، أ؛it`, which translates to "The Stormgod came". In both instances, `أ؛it` directly corresponds to "came".

The word `illuyanka-` appears multiple times, consistently referring to the "snake". The form `illuyankaإ،` is used when the snake is the subject of the sentence, as in `naإ،ta illuyankaإ، ... إ،arؤپ أ؛ؤ“r` ("And the snake came up...").

The word `naإ،ta` appears at the beginning of several sentences in the Hittite text. The corresponding English sentences all begin with "And" (e.g., "And he called up the snake...", "And the snake came up..."). This shows that `naإ،ta` is a conjunction meaning "and".

The word `إ،arؤپ` is used in `hatteإ،naz إ،arؤپ kalliإ،ta` ("called up from the hole") and `illuyankaإ،... إ،arؤپ أ؛ؤ“r` ("the snake came up"). In both cases, `إ،arؤپ` corresponds to the directional particle "up".

The word `ekuer` appears in `nuza eter ekuer` ("And they ate and drank") and `naإ،ta palhan hإ«mandan ekuer` ("And they drank all the kettles"). In both cases, the action is "drank", indicating `ekuer` means "drank".

By comparing the forms for "snake", we see `illuyankaإ،` is used for the subject ("the snake came up") while `illuyankan` is used for the direct object ("he called... the snake"). This contrast demonstrates that the suffix `-aإ،` marks the subject of a verb.

The suffix `-إ،a` is attached to nouns, as seen in `hupaإ،iyaإ،إ،a` ("And Hupasiyas") and `dingirmeإ،إ،a` ("and the gods"). It functions as an enclitic conjunction, adding the meaning of "and" to the word it attaches to.

The suffix `-meإ،` is found in `dumumeإ،إ،u` ("his sons") and `dingirmeإ،إ،a` ("and the gods"). The roots `dumu` (son) and `dingir` (god) are made plural by adding `-meإ،`. Therefore, it is a plural marker.

The suffix `-er/ؤ“r` appears on verbs like `أ؛ؤ“r` ("they came"), `eter` ("they ate"), `ekuer` ("they drank"), and `eإ،er` ("they were"). All of these verbs are in the past tense and have a 3rd person plural subject ("they" or a plural noun). This indicates it is the marker for the 3rd person plural past tense.

The form `illuyankan` is used when the snake is the direct object of a verb, as in `nu illuyankan ... kalؤ“liؤ“t` ("...and tied the snake"). This is in contrast to the subject form `illuyankaإ،`. Thus, `-an` is the marker for the direct object.
The phrase `adanna akuwanna` is translated as "to eat and to drink". These are infinitives. The common suffix `-anna` is therefore the marker of the infinitive.
The verb `أ؛it` ("came") is used with singular subjects like "Hupasiyas" and "The Stormgod". It is a past tense verb. The suffix `-it` therefore marks a 3rd person singular past tense verb.
